digest.editor_note: Fellow Humans, Brent crude near $120 a barrel, the Strait of Hormuz functionally closed, and Sen. Lindsey Graham — of all people — is urging Israel to show restraint. We are well past the point where any of this feels like a tail risk. The Iran war is now the central organizing fact of global markets, energy supply, and apparently the dollar's long-term viability, and the morning's reading suggests nobody in Washington has a firm grip on where this ends.

sentiment.label: Risk-Off
sentiment.context: Strait of Hormuz closure, Brent near $120/bbl, Saudi Aramco rerouting output, a $50B U.S. war supplemental incoming, and credible dollar reserve currency concerns dominate the tape.

stories.source: The Times of India / Economic Times
stories.headline: Saudi Aramco reroutes crude as Hormuz closure sends Brent near $120/bbl
stories.body: Saudi Aramco is diverting oil shipments to the Red Sea port of Yanbu after the Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed by the U.S.-Israel campaign against Iran, with Brent crude futures surging to near $120 per barrel. Multiple Middle Eastern producers are curtailing output amid the disruption. Analysts warn that elevated fuel prices could persist for months, with transport and food costs set to follow crude higher.

stories.headline: Even Lindsey Graham urges Israel to show caution after strikes on Iran fuel depots
stories.body: Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-SC), one of the most hawkish voices in Washington on Iran, publicly called on Israel to be more selective about its target selection after Saturday strikes hit 30 fuel storage sites and other energy infrastructure in Tehran, sparking toxic fires and acidic rain. The Trump administration, which received advance notice of the strikes, was reportedly caught off guard by their scale, with officials warning the attacks could push Iranians to rally behind the regime rather than against it. Graham simultaneously told Fox News that Operation Epic Fury would escalate further within two weeks.

stories.headline: Ron Paul warns Iran war cost — $891M/day and rising — threatens dollar reserve status
stories.body: The U.S. government is spending approximately $891.4 million per day on the Iran war according to CSIS estimates, with a $50 billion supplemental funding request expected to land in Congress shortly — a figure Paul calls a floor, not a ceiling. He argues the fiscal pressure will force the Federal Reserve to monetize debt at scale, accelerating foreign central bank diversification away from dollar-denominated assets. Paul warns a resulting loss of reserve currency status could trigger an economic contraction worse than the Great Depression.

stories.headline: Macron deploys Charles de Gaulle carrier group to Cyprus, pledges Hormuz escort mission
stories.body: French President Emmanuel Macron confirmed the deployment of the aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle and its battle group near Cyprus following drone and missile strikes on the island, declaring that "when someone attacks Cyprus, they attack all of Europe." Macron also announced plans for a "purely defensive" French naval force to escort tankers and commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, and called on other EU member states to join. The Mistral air-defense system and frigate Languedoc are already operational in the region.

stories.headline: NSE moves toward long-delayed $2.5 billion IPO, merchant banker appointments due in March
stories.body: India's National Stock Exchange is set to appoint merchant bankers this month for a potential $2.5 billion offer-for-sale, reviving a listing process that has been stalled for years by regulatory delays and governance concerns. The IPO would rank among the largest in Indian market history and would give public investors direct exposure to one of Asia's highest-volume exchange operators. Regulatory clearance from SEBI remains the outstanding variable.

stories.headline: First hydrogen fuel cell Class-8 drayage truck commissioned at 17,000 lb curb weight
stories.body: Savage Companies and Symbio North America have commissioned what they describe as the world's lightest zero-emission Class-8 tractor, a 4x2 hydrogen fuel cell electric drayage truck weighing approximately 17,000 pounds with full-shift range and no mid-shift refueling requirement. The vehicle uses a 150kW StackPack architecture paired with 34kg of onboard hydrogen storage at 70 MPa. Savage intends to use the platform as the basis for transitioning its 70-plus California drayage fleet to zero-emission operations.

stories.headline: India's MeitY seeks industry input on accelerating data protection compliance deadlines
stories.body: India's Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology is awaiting industry feedback on proposals to compress the compliance timeline under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, according to Secretary S. Krishnan. The move signals regulatory urgency as global peers tighten data governance frameworks and multinational firms operating in India face mounting uncertainty over implementation schedules. A shortened runway would put significant pressure on technology companies across the subcontinent to accelerate internal data infrastructure changes.

quote.text: The first panacea for a mismanaged nation is inflation of the currency; the second is war. Both bring a temporary prosperity; both bring a permanent ruin.
quote.attribution: Ernest Hemingway, Notes on the Next War, Esquire, 1935
